title: Ficino on the Daimon
subtitle: Commentarial perspectives on Ficino's concept of the daimon

summary.one_line: A compilation of scholarly commentary on Marsilio Ficino's concept of the daimon as tutelary spirit, ontological mediator, and object of astrological and magical practice.
summary.scope: Secondary scholarship only (Tier 2). No primary source passages from Ficino's own works. All passages come from academic commentaries on Ficino.

sources.label: Vieira — Marsílio Ficino sobre o daemon socrático
Kiosoglou — Marsilio Ficino and the Soul
Corrias — From Daemonic Reason to Daemonic Imagination

body.text: [Passage to be extracted from Vieira's analysis of Ficino's commentary on the Apology. Vieira argues that Ficino's daimon is fundamentally Socratic — a tutelary spirit assigned at birth, not a celestial intelligence or planetary angel.]
body.notes: Key source for the Socratic lineage of Ficino's daimon concept

body.text: [Passage to be extracted from Kiosoglou on Ficino's use of Proclus' Elements of Theology. Kiosoglou demonstrates that Ficino's daimon functions as the vehicle of the soul — an ontological intermediary between the immortal intellect and the mortal body, structurally derived from Proclean metaphysics.]
body.notes: Connects Ficino's daimon to the Proclean tradition of metaphysical intermediaries

body.text: [Passage to be extracted from Voss on Ficino's astrological framework. Voss shows that the daimon operates through planetary mediation — the natal configuration of the heavens determines the specific character and function of the daimon for each individual.]
body.notes: Establishes the astrological dimension of Ficino's daimon — not just a philosophical concept but an astrological reality

body.text: [Passage to be extracted from Voss on Ficino's musical therapy. Voss describes how Ficino used Orphic hymns and planetary songs to attune the soul to its daimon — a practical, therapeutic application of the daimonic principle.]
body.notes: Connects the daimon concept to Ficino's practical magical and musical practices

body.text: [Passage to be extracted from Corrias on the distinction between Ficino's daimon and Plotinus' daimon. Corrias argues that Ficino transforms the Plotinian daimon from a purely noetic entity into a personally accessible spiritual guide — a shift from reason to imagination.]
body.notes: SOURCE NOT YET ACQUIRED — paywalled. Passage to be filled when OA copy is found or paper is purchased.

gaps: Ficino's own definition of daimon (primary source needed)
Latin terminology analysis (genius vs daemon vs angelus)
Ficino's debt to Apuleius's De Deo Socratis
Ficino's planetary hour system for daimonic practice
Three Books on Life astrological passages (primary source)
Ficino's talismanic magic and the daimon
Comparison with Pico della Mirandola's daimon concept
Comparison with the Christian guardian angel tradition
The reception of the Socratic daimon in Renaissance thought
Ficino's sources for the Socratic daimon (Plato, Apuleius, Augustine)

issues.title: Missing Corrias comparison with Plotinus
issues.description: Corrias' paper on the Plotinus-Ficino comparison is paywalled. The comparison section currently has only placeholder text.
suggested_sources: Find OA copy of Corrias — From Daemonic Reason to Daemonic Imagination

issues.title: No primary source passages
issues.description: This RO currently contains only secondary commentary. No passages from Ficino's own works (Three Books on Life, Platonic Theology, Letters) are included.

issues.title: Voss and Kiosoglou may disagree on daimon-soul relationship
issues.description: Voss emphasizes the astrological and musical dimensions. Kiosoglou emphasizes the metaphysical and Proclean structure. These may not contradict — but the current RO doesn't check.

issues.title: Passages are placeholder text
issues.description: All passages currently contain bracketed descriptions instead of actual extracted text. Real passages need to be extracted from the PDFs.

versions.summary: Initial draft compilation from 3 secondary sources. All passages are placeholder text — extraction not yet performed.
